title: Soy container candle -- rose geranium and bergamot
subtitle: A floral-citrus soy wax candle with rose geranium and bergamot essential oils
excerpt: Rose geranium is one of the few floral essential oils affordable enough for candle use and stable enough to survive the pour. Bergamot adds a light citrus top note that lifts the heavy floral. Both perform well at low concentration in soy wax.
sourceNotes: Rose geranium and bergamot stability in soy wax at 55°C: candle formulation practice. Essential oil usage rates for floral and citrus oils in container candles: standard candle-craft reference.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Cure 48 hours for best hot throw.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Candles keep two years at room temperature. Discard if surface turns oily or fragrance has gone flat.

glossaryTerms.definition: Steam-distilled Pelargonium roseum oil. A floral, green-rosy note that is substantially more affordable than rose absolute. One of the most usable florals in candle making -- it holds better in wax than most single-note florals.

content.text: True rose essential oil is one of the most expensive in aromatherapy and performs poorly in candle wax -- most of its character evaporates during the pour.

is the practical choice for candle making: it has a rosy, green-floral scent, holds up better through the wax pour than true rose, and costs far less. Bergamot adds a citrus note that stops the geranium from being too heavy.

items.prepNote: about 5% of wax weight; about 140 drops
about 2% of wax weight; about 60 drops
CD-12 for a 6 to 7 cm jar

content.text: Stick the wick tab to the jar base and set the centring bar in place. Melt 140 g of soy wax to 70°C. Remove from the heat and cool to 57°C.
Add the rose geranium and bergamot essential oils by weight and stir slowly for 30 seconds. Pour steadily into the jar at 55 to 57°C. Fill to 1.5 cm below the rim. Leave undisturbed for two hours.
Trim the wick to 5 mm. Cure for 48 hours before lighting. The cold throw will be a light, pleasant floral; the hot throw develops more fully as the wax pool forms.

Allow the first burn to reach a full melt pool at the jar edges. Trim the wick before each subsequent burn. The bergamot top note will be most noticeable in the first 20 minutes of each burn; the deeper rose geranium base lingers for the full session.
